---
name: Brand Guidelines
description: Visual brand tokens for all motion graphics and infographics — colours, font, animation style, Remotion project location
type: user
---

Always apply these brand elements automatically to any motion graphic or infographic unless explicitly overridden.

## Colours
- Background: radial gradient `#e0e1dd` (centre) → `#6b6b6b` (mid) → `#222222` (edge)
- Primary ring / accent: `#415a77` (steel blue)
- Secondary ring / accent: `#c2a46d` (warm gold)
- Text primary: `#1D1D1F`
- Text secondary: `rgba(29,29,31,0.45)`
- Text muted: `rgba(29,29,31,0.6)`
- Text accent: `#415a77`

## Typography
- Font: Montserrat Extra Bold (weight 800) — all display text, no exceptions

## Animation (Remotion)
- Duration: 3 seconds for callout/stat graphics
- In: spring scale 0.84→1 + staggered left-to-right reveals
- Out: Gaussian blur dissolve (blur 0→24px + opacity 1→0, 0.8s, starts at 2.2s)
- Springs: snappy `{damping:15, stiffness:220}` / bouncy `{damping:12, stiffness:280}` / smooth `{damping:14, stiffness:200}`

## Brand Token File
All tokens in `C:\Users\mcopp\focus-remotion\src\brand.ts` — always import from there for new compositions, never hardcode values.

**Why:** User said time is their biggest asset — brand consistency must be automatic, not something they fiddle with per project.

message.content:
Thank you. This is my new script, I need infographics for this project. I need it to follow brand rules please: Most event testimonials I have seen try to achieve a specific goal which is to try and sell or resell tickets but if they aren’t done right the effect of your video won’t land the way you want it to. So, in this video I am going to take you through some of the key pillars of filming a testimonial and the best part is if you follow this framework it can often mask poorer video quality. So, if you don’t have the budget to hire a videographer which can sometimes cost upwards of two thousand pounds, you can still benefit from this as well. If you’re unsure what a testimonial video is or how to film one watch this until the end and it will set you up well for your next event. 
So, you've probably seen them yourself: an attendee standing in a noisy hallway saying, “The catering was great and I met some nice people and what a fantastic time it was”' While the attendee isn’t wrong in saying that, attendees are often unaware of what it is you the event host is trying to achieve by filming them in the first place and so will find themselves saying things that will shine a light on how they felt opposed to pinpointing the things that measurably increased their chances of signing a term sheet with another firm. And so the unintended consequence is that your attempts to reconvert them later won't work the way you want.
Just remember it isn’t the high-level decision makers that we are trying to influence, it’s the individual people in charge of a firm's marketing budget that ultimately make decisions and so, as much as I hate to say it, “nice people” or people appearing nice is a variable and what they need is something more concrete like proof of Outcome. According to Wyzowl’s 2026 data, 91% of B2B buyers say that result-driven stories are the primary factor in their purchasing decisions, so if your testimonials aren't structured in the format that they are looking for, it will often just appear as “white noise" to them, which isn’t what we are looking for. So, before I show you how to film one, I just want to quickly explain what a testimonial actually is. 
A structured testimonial acts as a 'Risk Mitigator.' It proves that the success of your event is repeatable. It takes a lot of the 'guesswork' out of the booking or sometimes rebooking process. So, by capturing the right story, you are giving your sponsors the evidence they need to say 'Yes' this is exactly what I need for next year" or “No, perhaps not, this isn’t really what I am looking for”. Okay, so how do we structure our testimonial?
3. The Strategy: The "Problem-Shift-Result" Framework
"To get a high-performing testimonial, doon’t ask questions like “Did you enjoy the event?” and instead start using the PSR Framework, The Problem, The Shift, and The Result:
The Problem: What was the specific hurdle your firm was facing before you arrived? So, for some firms that might be something like they couldn't get a meeting with a different firm, or you might get a results based answer. Once they answer you can then ask:

The Shift: What happened at the forum that changed that? Ideally, you should be hearing answers like “The structured networking allowed us to have a 20-minute face-to-face” or “The 1-to-1 partnering system allowed us to bypass the “Gatekeepers” and have a real conversation about our clinical data.” Both of those are good answers and that’s more or less the kind of thing you want to be aiming for with your question and answers in your testimonial. This is an example of a testimonial with a firm I recently worked with.

The Result: Once you have those two done, just end on  What their measurable outcome was. Don’t ask the question using those exact words, try and be a bit more pliable if you can. Asking what their results were is good enough. So, you might get an answer like “We are now in the second stage of due diligence.” While most people hate industry jargon, this is one of those few occasions where jargon can be helpful.
So, now that we have that out the way, let’s move on to actually filming it. How do you do it, what do you need, and do you need any experience? 
I’m interrupting this for a moment because I don't believe in 'subtle' marketing. I run Foundry Media, and we offer a Done-For-You or Done-With-You service specifically for forum organizers.
I know the pain: You spend a year building a world-class event, only for the ROI to become invisible the moment the doors close and for the momentum to disappear.. We solve that by combining high-end video production with a targeted online marketing strategy that reaches the accounts you need it to. We don't just capture the moment; we make sure your sponsors and attendees see the value of your event throughout the year.
Here is my guarantee: If we sit down for a 1-1 strategy session and the plan I build for you isn't SMART—meaning it isn't Smart, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, and Timebound - I will give you a full refund for the session, no questions asked. I’m interested in engineering results, not just filming footage. Now, back to the strategy.
If you’re going to film this yourself, that is absolutely fine. You can achieve a lot using a smart phone or a professional camera. If you remember what I said earlier, you can get away with poorer video quality as long as you’re hitting all the right notes. That’s not to say that you shouldn’t aim for better production quality where you can, but if you absolutely can’t it’s okay you just use a phone if you need to. Try and use a tripod to keep things steady and if you can, film in front of the logo of the host organiser. Whatever you do, do not do this… As you can see, I was filming in front of a projector screen. I didn’t want to and the event host was adamant that this is what she wanted so I had little choice in the matter. I understand why she wanted me to do this though, it’s because she wanted the logo in the picture and I do get it. But wherever possible, do not film facing another light source. It interferes with the cameras sensor and wether you’re using a professional camera or a smart phone, the sensor will often expose for the brightest lightsource making whoever you’re filming extremely dark compared to the rest of the room. While there are ways to fix this for a lot of people who are not professional filmmakers are likely going to run into issues messing up your recording. You’ll want to make sure you’re getting the correct exposure from the beginning because fixing it in post production can be tricky, lead to poor quality or loss of quality, and so that’s why I am showing you how to do it this way - because it will save most people a whole bunch of time later down the line. 
If you have a spot in mind, try and light up whoever it is speaking with some additional light. You don’t need pro-grade lights, you can get away with using these tiny pocket-sized lights. They are inexpensive, I think these cost me around twenty pounds each, and if you use three of them, you’ll find that whoever it is that’s speaking is speaking will be lit quite nicely. Place 1 light on a tripod and lift it to head height forty five degrees from the centre of the speaker's face. Take a second light and place it on the opposite side of the speaker ninety degrees from the centre of the speaker's face. Lastly, take the third light and place it forty five degrees behind the speaker on either side. This will light them up well. If you use these RGB lights that I bought, you’ll be able to adjust the colour and strength of the light to your needs. They are really easy to use and have a five minute learning time. They are so easy you don’t even need the instructions. Well worth getting in my opinion.
Lastly is sound, make sure that your speaker is well heard. Don’t rely on the microphone inside your smart phone or professional camera. If you have to use it and it can’t be avoided, go for it. It isn’t ideal but if it is all you have to work with that’s okay. The difference can be night and day though. In fact, a lot of people can be really forgiving when it comes to how a video looks. So, if the video looks like it’s not the highest quality, it’s shaking, and the moments are smooth, as long as it sounds good your audience will generally be quite forgiving. This is an example of how big the difference can be… To do this without a professional sound desk, just use a simple mic, no need for anything fancy. If you pick up a DJI or Rhodes microphone from amazon you’ll be surprised at just how incredible the noise cancelling quality and recording quality is. If you’re recording in a very busy area which is likely to be the case for most forums, this will be a life saver. Just plug it into your professional camera or smart phone and as long as you see the levels moving up and down on your transmitter, you’re good to go. Really easy to use, slightly technical but in no way anything a person with no experience couldn’t figure out in about 30 minutes of tinkering around. There are plenty of YouTube videos about how to use microphones like this. Also, just in case you have no technical experience recording using a microphone, if you use any microphone, that sound quality is baked directly into your footage, so you won’t have to do any editing to it at all. It will all sound excellent straight out of the gate. 
And that is pretty much it. When you film your events, just remember that while you aren't looking for 'smiles', it is always nice to capture them on camera; just make sure that if you’re looking for content that you can specifically use to market ticket sales, you’re also looking for data points. This is how you build a library of proof that makes your forum appear as the undisputed leader in your niche."
Hopefully you’ve found this helpful and if it hasn’t or if you have any questions that you want answered feel free to ask either by DMing me or drop a comment in the comment section I am usually quite quick to respond but life does sometimes get in the way so if you do leave a comment below and I don’t reply just DM me and I’ll answer your question there and I’ll see you on the next one, bye bye for now.

Let me read the script carefully and identify the graphic-worthy moments before proposing anything.

Here's what I'd pull out as compositions for Script 14:

---

**Stat callouts (standard GraphicWrapper, 323 frames)**
1. **NinetyOnePercent** — "91% of B2B buyers say result-driven stories are the primary factor in their purchasing decisions" — Wyzowl 2026
2. **VideographerCost** — "Upwards of £2,000" — cost of hiring a videographer

**Framework graphics (staggered reveals)**
3. **PSRFramework** — 3-step: Problem → Shift → Result
4. **RiskMitigator** — "A Structured Testimonial Acts As A Risk Mitigator" + 3 sub-points (repeatable success / removes guesswork / gives sponsors evidence to decide)
5. **SMARTGuarantee** — 5-part acronym: Specific / Measurable / Achievable / Realistic / Timebound

**Diagram**
6. **ThreeLightSetup** — top-down lighting diagram: key light 45° front, fill light 90° side, backlight 45° behind

---

That's 6 compositions. PSRFramework, RiskMitigator, and SMARTGuarantee would be staggered multi-element builds. ThreeLightSetup would be a custom diagram.

Does that match what you had in mind, or do you want to add/drop any? Also — should ThreeLightSetup be a visual diagram (SVG-style overhead view) or a simple text list of the 3 positions?
Let me read a few existing compositions to match the patterns exactly before building.

### Script 12 render scripts (package.json)
- `npm run render:prores:s12` — renders all four at 4K ProRes 4444
- Individual: `render:prores:s12:areyourunning`, `render:prores:s12:realestateagency`, `render:prores:s12:highfrequencyforex`, `render:prores:s12:sportsrecruiters`

### TextWipeBase.tsx
Shared component used by RealEstateAgency, HighFrequencyForex, SportsRecruiters.
Props: `line1`, `line2`, `fontSize`. Transparent background, ProRes 4444 alpha.
Animation: line1 bounces in (SPRING.bouncy), line2 slides up at frame 12, divider grows at frame 26.

## Technical Notes
- Project: 1920×1080 @ 30fps (compositions stay at 1080p in Root.tsx)
- 4K output: achieved at render time via `--scale 2` flag (→ 3840×2160) — do NOT change composition dimensions
- ProRes 4K renders: `--codec prores --prores-profile 4444 --scale 2`
- Standard compositions: 323 frames (9 frames in + 10s dwell + 14 frames out)
- Complex stagger compositions (ProductionTimeline, ProductionEffort): custom durations — last element + 300 frames dwell + 14 frames out
- Rule: dwell = 10 seconds from last element on screen
- Background gradient centre stop: 16% (set in brand.ts)
- All compositions registered in Root.tsx
- All import from `./brand` — never hardcode values
- Total: 69 .tsx files in src/ (including Root.tsx, GraphicWrapper.tsx, GraphicWrapperLong.tsx, index.tsx, TextWipeBase.tsx)
- New script render scripts in package.json must include `--scale 2` for 4K output
- Overlay/alpha compositions: use transparent background + ProRes 4444 — do NOT use GraphicWrapper for these
- Always use `npm run render:prores:s12` (or equivalent script) rather than Studio render button for 4K

message.content: Lets also make a 2D and then a 3D visual of the lights and a face. Doesn't have to be a detailed face. Lets just try a present a clear dynamic image of what this will look like in practice. In fact, for the 3D version, lets have a room with 4 walls. The camera zooms into the wall closest to camera. The wall closest to camera lifts up. We are presented with a speaker standing in a venue with other people in the background. The speaker is speaking to a camera. The camera is on a tripod. The speaker is surrounded by the 3 light set up I have described. And then the infographic can end like the other do.

content.text: API Error: Claude's response exceeded the 32000 output token maximum. To configure this behavior, set the CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS environment variable.

I think "What was the issue?" is asking me to flag concerns before building — so here's my honest assessment:

**2D version — straightforward.** SVG top-down diagram: circle for the speaker's head, camera icon in front, three light sources at the correct angles with dotted lines and labels. Clean, clear, very doable.

**3D version — ambitious but buildable.** Here's where it gets complex:

- The **room with 4 walls + zoom + front wall lifting** is achievable with CSS 3D perspective and `rotateX` transforms — I've done similar things in this project
- The **speaker, camera on tripod, and light stands** would all be stylised SVG shapes — think bold geometric silhouettes, not detailed illustrations. The "people in background" would be simple dark silhouettes to suggest a busy venue
